text: CANGIA, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Albania, near the r. bank of the Lura or Rogus, and 11 m. W of Arta. CANGREJOR, CANGRAY, or CRAB ISLAND, a small deltoid island of the Orinoco, in N lat. 8° 45', W long. 60° 15'. It is inhabited by Caribbean Indians.
CANHA, a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, comarca and 35 m. NE of Setubal, on a hill near the l. bank of a-river of the same name, and 4 m. SSE of its junction with the Almansor. Pop. 2,000.
CANIAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot, cant. of La Bastide. Pop. 1,085.

CANIAPUSCAW, or Koksoak River, a river of British North America, in Labrador, which issues from a lake of the same name; runs N; receives on the r. the Wauquash, and on the l. the Nutwakame or Kenoganisse rivers; and falls into Ungava or South bay in N lat. 59°.

CANICATTI, or CANIGETTI, a town of Sicily, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. and 15 m. ENE of Girgenti. near the r. bank of the Naro or Hyspa. Pop. 13,886. In the environs are extensive sulphurworks.
CANICUL, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, near the r. bank of the Madris, 3 m. SSE of the junction of that river with the Hinter- Rhein, and 25 m. SSW of Chur.
CANIDOLE, two small islets in the Adriatic, to the W of Osero. They rise to about 25 ft. above sealevel.
CANIE ISLANDS, a group of islands in the estuary of the St. Lawrence, in N lat. 49° 51', W long. 66° 42'.

CANIGOU (LE), a mountain of France, in the dep. of the Pyrenees-Orientales, cant. and 8 m. S of Prades. It forms a spur of the Pyrenees; and is one 38 of the loftiest summits of that chain, rising in some of its heads to 9,500 ft. above sea-level. It separates the valley of the Tech from that of the Teta.
CANIHUEL, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Côtes-du-Nord, cant. of Bothoa, 18 m. S of Guingamp. Pop. 1,526.

CANILES, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 55 m. ENE of Granada, and 3 m. S of Baza, at the confluence of the Galopon with the Balaja. Pop. 4,234. It is supposed to be of Arabic origin; and contains a parish-church, an hospital, and a convent.
CANILLAS DE ACEITUNO, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, prov. and 22 m. NE of Malaga, and 11 m. N of Velez-Malaga. Pop. 2,247. It contains a parish-church, and a convent.

CANINDE', a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Piauhi, which takes its rise in the Serra dos Oous Irmaos; runs NNW; receives the Itahim on the r., and the Piauhi on the l.; and after a total course of 125 m., falls into the Parahiba, 70 m. NW of Oeiras.
CANINDE, or Porto-das-Piranhas, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Alagoas, on the l. bank of the Sao-Francisco, 10 m. S of the Serra Olho-d'Agua, and 50 m. below the fall of Paulo-Affonso.

CANINO, a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the delegation and 20 m. WNW of Viterbo, and 9 m. SW of Lake Bolsena. It contains a fine palace, belonging to the family of Lucien Bonaparte, and has some iron-works.

CANISBAY, a parish in Caithness, at the extremity of the mainland of Scotland, bounded on the N by the Pentland frith, and on the E by the German ocean. The coast-line, about 18 m. in extent, is exceedingly bold. The N coast has little of that stupendous boldness for which the E one is remarkable. The bays upon the coast are those of Gills, Duncansby, and Freswick; in all of which, if the weather be moderate, vessels can lie in safety and take in their cargoes, but none of them are eligible stations in rough weather. The Warth hill, on the E coast, is of considerable height and magnitude, but is the only CAN Moy, in the NW corner of the parish, is about 2 m. in circumf. The principal public roads in the district are those leading from Houna S to Wick, and W to Thurso. The distance from Wick to Houna is 16 m.; and from Houna to Thurso 18 m. From Houna the ferry-boat crosses with the mails for Orkney every Tuesday, Thursday, and Saturday. From Burwick in South Ronaldshay, the Orkney mails in like manner cross to Houna. The distance—being the shortest betwixt Caithness and Orkney - is reckoned 12 m. If a passenger goes along with the mails, the freight is 1s.; but if he hires the ferry-boat for himself, it is 10s. Sometimes the communication even with the adjacent island of Stroma, is suspended for weeks. In the summer season there is almost a continued communication betwixt Caithness and Orkney in the traffic of horses. Pop. in 1801, 1,986; in 1831, 2,364; in 1841, 2,306. The island of Stroma, in the Pentland frith, about 3 m. from the shore, with a pop. in 1841 of 186, belongs to this parish.

CANISTEO, a township of Steuben co., in the state of New York, U.S., 14 m. SW of Bath, and 243 m. WSW of Albany. It presents a hilly surface, and is watered by a river of the same name and its tributaries. The hills are thickly wooded with pine. The soil is fertile, consisting to a great extent of clay and calcareous loam, and near the rivers is extremely rich. The C. river rises in Alleghany co., and unites with the Tioga in Steuben co., after a course of 50 m., 40 of which are boatable. CANISTER, GREAT, LITTLE, and WEST, islands of the Indian ocean, in the archipelago of Mergui, or Tenasserim, in N lat. 12° 52', and E long. 97° 52'; N lat. 12° 44′, and E long. 97° 56′; and N lat. 12° 37', and E long. 97° 28', respectively.

CANISY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Manche, arrond. of Saint-Lo. The cant. comprises 11 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 10,071. The town is 5 m. SW of Saint- Lo. Pop. 927. It possesses manufactories of cloth and ticking. - Also a hamlet in the dep. of the Somme, cant. of Nesle, and com. of Hombleux. Pop. 220.

CANIZA (SANTA TERESA DE), a town of Spain, in Galicia, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 24 m. SE of Pontevedra, in a mountainous district on the Achas, 20 m. NE of Tuy. Pop. 312. Its origin is of unknown antiquity.
CANJAYAR, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 18 m. from Almeria, in the Sierra de Gador. Pop. 2,700.
CANJE, a river of British Guiana, in the co. of Berbice, which runs N, and falls into the estuary of the Berbice, at New Amsterdam.
CANN, or Shaston St. Rumbold, a parish of Dorset, 14 m. SE of Shaftesbury, on the post-road. Area 910 acres. Pop. in 1841, 523.

CANNA, one of the four islands of the Hebrides which form the parish of Small Isles in Argyleshire. It is 3 m. NW of Rum, and 12 m. SW of the nearest point of Skye; half-way between the S extremity of Islay and the Butt of Lewis. It is about 43 m. long and 1 m. broad; and contains, with the contiguous island of Sanday, 429 arable acres, and 1,794 acres of green pasture. Its surface is partly high and rocky, but in no place rises more than 800 ft. above sea-level. On the SE side of C. lies Sanda, or Sanday, separated by a channel which is dry at low water. Between this island and C. lies the wellknown and much frequented harbour of C., 30 m. distant from that of Eigg. C. contained 304 inhabitants in 1796; 264 in 1831; and 255 in 1841. The pop. are all Catholics.

CANNA, a village of Naples, in the prov. of one in the parish deserving the name. The loch of | Calabria-Citra, district and 29 m. NE of Castrovillari, and 5 m. NE of Oriolo. Pop. 1,700. It has an annual fair. CANNAT (SAINT), OF SAINT-CANNAT-DE-SANZET, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Bouches, cant. of Lambese, 12 m. NW of Aix. Pop. 1,655. The environs produce considerable quantities of wine.

CANNE, a village of Naples, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Bari, district and 8 m. WSW of Barletta, near the S bank of the Ofanto. It occupies the site of the ancient Cannæ of the Romans, celebrated for the victory of Hannibal in 216 B. C., and was formerly the seat of a bishopric. The battle-field still retains the name of the Campo di Sangue.

CANNES, a canton, commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Var, arrond. of Grasse. The cant. comprises 6 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 8,610. The town is situated on the gulf of Napoule, 9 m. SE of Grasse, and 22 m. E of Draguignan, on the road from Toulon to Nice. Pop. 3,881. A battery on the shore forms its only defence, the fortress which commanded the town being now in ruins. It is well and regularly built, and a fine promenade extends along the coast. The harbour is small and shallow, and larger vessels are consequently obliged to anchor at a considerable distance from the shore. It possesses, however, some building docks, and an active export trade in oil, fruit, soap, perfumery, and salted provisions. Its chief imports are grain and salt. The locality produces olive-oil, oranges, figs, &c., but is very unhealthy. It contains several tanneries, and in the vicinity are extensive fisheries of anchovies and sardine. It was at this port that Napoleon landed on his escape from Elba on the 1st March, 1815.

CANNETELLO, a village of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria Ultra, district and 10 m. N of Reggio, on the strait of Messina. Pop. 2,230. It was nearly altogether destroyed by an earthquake in 1783. The adjacent fisheries are highly productive.

CANNETO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, delegation and 20 m. W of Mantua, pleasantly situated on the l. bank of the Oglio. Pop. 2,800. It is of great antiquity. A fair is held here once a-year.—Also a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of the Terra-di- Bari, district and 11 m. S of Bari. Pop. 2,192. It has a fair once a-year. The circondario produces almonds, anise and cummin in great abundance.
CANNET-PRES-CANNES (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Var, cant. of Cannes, 9 m. SE of Grasse. Pop. 1,480.
CANNET-PRES-LE-LUC (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Var, cant. of Le-Luc, 13 m. SSW of Draguignan. Pop. 1,004.

CANNING, a small island near the E coast of East or Old Greenland, at the entrance of Davy's sound, in N lat. 71° 50', and W long. 21° 30'.—Also a river of W Australia, flowing into the S side of the estuary of the Swan river. It descends from the Darling range, and flows NW. It is navigable only a few miles.

CANNINGTON, a parish of Somerset, 32 m. NW of Bridgewater, S of the Parret, and near the Bristol and Exeter railway. Area 3,970 acres. Pop. in 1841, 1,349. In the vicinity is Kithill, 1,067 ft. in alt. above sea-level.

CANNOBIE, a parish in Dumfries-shire, about 9 m. in length, and 6 m. in breadth; containing 23,000 acres. It may be considered as the low lands of Eskdale; for its highest grounds, which rise gradually to the E and NE, as contrasted with the elevated peaks in the conterminous parishes, cannot be called mountains; at the same time the surface is diversified by ridges and flats, excepting the haughs on the banks of the Esk. The central part is intersected CAN by the Esk, and this parish is also watered by the Liddel which divides it from England, and the Tarras, remarkable for its rugged channel and romantic scenery. Freestone, limestone, and coal, are abundant. There is a strong chalybeate spring at Heathet on the Cumberland side of the Liddel, and a spring on the banks of the Tarras which has a petrifying quality. Pop. in 1801, 2,580; in 1831, 2,997; in 1841, 3,032.

CANNOCK, a parish and township of Staffordshire, 5 m. SE of Penkridge, near the Stafford canal and the Birmingham and Liverpool railway. Area 11,970 acres. Pop. in 1841, 2,852. In the vicinity is the celebrated Cannock-Chace, comprising an area extending to the Trent, of 25,000 acres.
CANNOCK-WOOD, a township of Staffordshire, in the p. of Cannock. Pop. in 1841, 275.

CANNON, or Canon, a river of the United States, in the Sioux territory, which runs ENE, and falls into the Mississippi, between lakes St. Croix and Pepin, in N lat. 44° 38′, after a course of about 90 m.

CANNON, a county in the state of Tennessee, U. S., comprising a superficies of about 100 sq. m., drained by affluents of Cumberland river. It is hilly, but possesses a fertile soil. Pop. in 1840, 7,193, of whom 6,558 were whites, 618 slaves, and 17 free-coloured. Cap., Woodbury.

CANNON BALL RIVER, a river of the United States, in Mandan district, which takes its rise in the Black hills, and unites with the Missouri, 60 m. SSW of Fort Mandan, in N lat. 46° 20', after a total course, through a fine valley, in an ENE direction, of about 135 m. Its banks consist of rich alluvium.
CANNONBY-CROSS, a parish and township of Cumberland, 7 m. NNW of Cockermouth, and near the Carlisle railway. Area 2,400 acres. Pop. in 1841, 5,731.
CANNONSBURG, a town of Washington co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., on Chartiers creek, 18 m. SW of Pittsburg. Pop. in 1840, 687.
CANNOUCHEE, a river in the state of Georgia, U. S., the most westerly and largest of the affluents of the Ogeechee. Its total course is 140 m., of which 50 are navigable.
CANO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alentejo, 26 m. SW of Portalegre, and 7 m. W of Souzel.
CANOA, a town of New Grenada, in the of Eguador, 5 m. SE of Cape Pasado, and 115 m. WSW of Quito.—Also a river which unites with the Coca, 5 m. SE of the junction of the Napo.
CANOA, or Arraial-da-Canoa, a small town of Brazil, in the prov. of Parahiba, district of Pianco, on a river of the same name.
CANOA POINT, a promontory of New Grenada, on the coast of Guayaquil, 10 m. SSE of Cape S. Lorenzo, in S lat. 1° 10'.

CANOBBIO, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Tessin, district and 21 m. NNE of Lugano, and 11 m. SE of Bellinzone. Pop. (Catholics) 206. It contains the ruins of a castle, and has several paper-mills, and iron and copper-works. - Also a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 22 m. NNE of Palanza, on the W bank of Lake Maggiore, at the mouth of the Tenere. Pop. 2,000. It contains a fine church, and is celebrated for its tanneries.
CANOEIRAS, a numerous tribe of aborigines, who inhabit the banks of the Tocantins, in Brazil.
CANOGA, a village of Fayette township, in Seneca co., state of New York, U. S., about a mile W of Cayuga lake, and 173 m. W of Albany. Pop. in 1840, 500.
CANOINHAS, a stream in Brazil, an affluent of the Curitiba, which forms a partial boundary line between the provinces of Sao - Paulo and Santa- Catharina.
CANOLO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria Ultra, district and 4 m. NNW of Gerace, on the E side of the Apennines. Pop. 1,500. Silk worms are extensively cultivated in the environs.
CANOLOOR, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, in the Banda sea, in S lat. 5° 20', and E long. 132° 15′.

CANOMA, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, which takes its rise in the Parentintins territory, runs N, inosculates with the Mataura on the l., passes through lake Canoma, or Uraria, which has an outlet to the Madeira, thence bends NE, receiving on the r. the Abacaxis, Apinguiribo, Mauheguacu, Massary, and Tupinambaranas, and unites with the Amazon 3 m. N of Villa da Rainha, and 155 m. below the junction of the Madeira. It is known in the latter half of its course by the various names of the Furo, Abacaxis, and Mauhe.
CANOMIA, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Thessaly, on the gulf of Salonica, 13 m. NNE of Hegia, and 5 m. NW of Cape Kissovo.
CANON-FROME, a parish of Herefordshire, 6 m. NNW of Ledbury, on the Frome. Area 1,040 acres. Pop. in 1841, 115.

CANONICA, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, delegation and 10 m. SW of Bergamo, on the r. bank of the Adda, at the junction of the Brembo and of the canal of Martezana. Pop. trade. Aureolus was here conquered by the Goths, and died in 247 in the vicinity.
CANONICUT, an island of the United States, in Narraganset bay, 3 m. W of Rhode island. It is 7 m. long, and about 1 m. in breadth.
CANON-PION, or Pyon, a parish of Herefordshire, 4 m. SW of Weobly. Area 3,730 acres. Pop. in 1841, 681.
CANOPI, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of the Rio Negro, which unites with the Caqueta or Hyapura, 130 m. NW of the confluence of that river with the Amazon.

CANOSA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Terra di Bari, cap. of a circondario in the district and 15 m. WSW of Barletta, and 3 m. S of the Ofanto. Pop. 8,080. This town—the Canusium of the Romans—of which numerous remains still exist, contains an ancient cathedral, in which is the tomb of Bohëmund, prince of Antioch, a parish church, &c. It has two annual fairs.
CANOSIO, a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 25 m. WNW of Coni, on an affluent of the Maira.
CANOSSA, a town of Modena, in the district and 14 m. SW of Reggio, on a mountain. It contains a castle in which Henry IV. performed the penance imposed upon him by Gregory VII.
CANOUAN, an island of the West Indies, in the Grenadines group, in N lat. 12° 43', and W long. 62° 38′.

CANOURGUE (LA), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Lozere, arrond. of Marvejols. The cant. comprises 7 com., and in 1831 contained 6,479 inhabitants. The town is situated in a fertile valley on the Urugne, 11 m. S of Marvejols, and 17 m. SW of Mende. Pop. 1,850. It possesses manufactories of cloth, caddis, calico, linen, &c., and cotton-mills, and has a considerable commerce in grain and cattle. CANOUGE, KANOGE, or KUNOGE, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 130 m. E of Agra, district and 70 m. ENE of Etawah, on the r. bank of the Calli Nudi, and connected also with the Ganges by a canal two miles in length, in N lat. 27° 4', and E long. 79° 47'. This town, which is of remote antiquity, consists now of but a single street, but the ruins of the ancient Canyacubja are still to CAN be found extending over an area of six miles. Excepting, however, portions of the city wall, and a small rude pagoda, adorned with images of Hindoo pantheism, no architectural memorial of the ancient capital of Hindostan is now discoverable amid the masses of rubbish which mark its site,—the mosques, tombs, and shrine of Mucdoom Jehaneea being of Mussulman origin. The latter are all built of stone, and in a tolerably good state of preservation. A little to the N of the town is a noble serai, and immediately adjacent the handsome modern mausoleum of Meerun, its founder. This town, supposed to be the Calinipaxa of Pliny, was formerly one of the most flourishing in Hindostan, and at the period of the Mahommedan invasion, was the cap. of a powerful empire. In 1018 it was taken by the celebrated Mahmoud of Ghizneh, but was subsequently conquered by the chief of Delhi and his confederates, when Mahmoud perished amidst its ruins. It never after recovered its political importance, but it is now the seat of a considerably active trade. The Hindostance language is said to have been originally spoken here in the greatest purity. The name Canyacubja — from canya, 'damsel,' and cubja, 'a spinal curve'-relates to a celebrated story of Hindu mythology. The surrounding district is sandy, and often suffers extremely from drought.

CANROODY, or KANROODY, a small district of 1,200. It is the entrepot of a considerable transit | Hindostan, a subdivision of the prov. of Gundwana, bounded on the S by the district of Sohagepoor, and intersected by the Sone and its tributaries. The cap., an unimportant town, is 17 m. N of Sohagepur.

CANSEAU, or Canso Cape, a headland of Nova Scotia, at the NE extremity of the peninsula, in N lat. 45° 18', and W long. 61°. A little to the NW is a small island of the same name, which contains an excellent harbour, capable of affording safe and commodious anchorage to vessels of the largest dimensions.

CANSEAU, or Canso (GUT OF), or BOYAU-DECANSEAU, a strait of British North America, which separates Nova Scotia from the island of Cape Breton, and which forms the principal entrance to the gulf of St. Lawrence. It is about 21 m. in length, extending from St. George's bay on the NW, to Chedabucto bay on the SE; and is 1 m. in average breadth. The scenery on each side is magnificently mountainous.
CANSTADT, or KANNSTADT, a town of Wurtemburg, in the circle and on the r. bank of the Neckar, 4 m. NE of Stuttgardt.

CANTA, a province of Peru, in the intendancy of Lima, bounded on the N by the prov. of Caxatamba; on the E by that of Pasco; on the S by the prov. of Huarochiri; and on the W by that of Chancay comprising an area of 105 m. in length from E to W, and 72 m. in breadth from N to S. Pop. 12,150. It is intersected by the Andes, and is watered by the Chancay and other considerable streams. It produces legumes and fruit, and affords excellent pasturage to numerous herds of cattle. Game is plentiful, and the mountain acclivities abound with lamas and vicumas or wild goats. The cap., of the same name, is situated on the E side of the Andes, 63 m. ENE of Chancay, and 75 m. NNE of Lima.
CANTABRANO, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 26 m. NNE of Burgos, and 13 m. NNW of Briviesca, on the l. bank of the Cadrecha.

CANTABRIAN MOUNTAINS, a range in Spain, forming the W prolongation of the Pyrenees, and running nearly under the parallel of 43° N, and between the meridians of 2° and 9° 30' W. They separate a part of Navarre from Guipuscoa, Biscay from Alava, the partido of Burgos from that of Montana, and the Asturias from Leon; and divide in Galicia, into two principal branches, one of which terminates in Cape Ortegal, and the other in Cape Finistere. From the sources of the Anezo, the E affluent of the Oria, where the Pyrenean chain may be regarded as terminating, to the Col de Gorritz, the C. chain runs parallel to the coast of the bay of Biscay, at a mean distance of 12 or 15 m. It then turns, nearly at a right angle, towards the S, and after running about 20 m. in that direction, abruptly turns to the N, and forms an elevated plateau on which the Minho has its head-streams. From this plateau it again descends towards the S, and again suddenly remounts to the N, and terminates on Cape Finistere. The other branch separates from the main range in about 43° 27', and 7° 28' W long., and after running to the W, turns N, and terminates in Cape Ortegal. The principal portions of the C. range, commencing at the W extremity of the Pyrenees are: 1st. The mountains of Aralar, between Navarre and Guipuscoa. -2d. The Sierra Arangazu, from which the three rivers Araquil, Oria, and Zadorra, respectively flow E, N, and W.-3d. The mountains of Altube, between Biscay and Alava.—4th. The Sierra Salvada in Biscay and Old Castile.—5th. The Sierra Angana in Old Castile, between the cols of Espinosa and Reynosa. - 6th. The Sierra Sejos, between the Col de Reynosa and the sources of the Ebro and the Pisuerga.-7th. The Sierra Cabadonga in Old Castile, the Asturias, and Leon, extending from the sources of the Pisuerga to those of the Esla.—8th. The mountains of Asturias, the Mons Vidius of the Romans, across which runs the grand road from Madrid to La Corogne.—9th. The Sierra Constantina in Galicia, extending from the l. bank of the Nogales to Mount Pico.—10th. The Sierra de Meyra, extending from Mount Pico to the sources of the Minotelo.—11th. The Sierra de Troncedo, or Mondonedo, in Galicia, extending from the Minotelo to the source of the Masma.—12th. The Sierra de Quadramon, running along the r. bank of the Ladra to the confluence of the Parga.—13th. The Sierra de Tecyra, which extends from the N of Mendo to Cape Finistere, and presents three cols or passes. Five inferior branches diverge from the C. chain. The first of these, beginning at the E, is the Maribi mountains, which separate the basin of the E Deba from that of the Durango.—The 2d, under the name of Villa Mayor, runs along the r. bank of the Nalon to the sources of the Sella. - The 3d, known as Mount Aramo, runs between the Caudal and the Trubia.— The 4th, the Sierra de Couto, runs along the r. bank of the Piguena to its confluence with the Narcea.- The 5th is the Sierra Marta, which separates the basins of the Nalon and the Navia.
CANTAGALLO, a parish and village of Tuscany, in the comp. of Florence, 16 m. N of Prato, in the Apennines. Pop. 329.

CANTAGALLO, a town of Brazil, cap. of the comarca of the same name, in the prov. and 90 m. NE of Rio-de-Janeiro, and 62 m. W of Campos. Pop. 4,000. It contains a circular plaza, in which, between two parallel streets, is the parish-church. The inhabitants reside chiefly in the suburbs. The comarca comprises also the parishes of Sumidouro, Santa-Rita, and Sao-Francisco de Paula, with a total pop. of 12,000. It is intersected by the Paquequera, Bosarahi, Grande, and Bengualas, affluents of the Parahiba, which latter river separates the district on the N from the prov. of Minas-Geraes. The productions of the district are conveyed on mules to the Macacu, for transit to the cap. Since the exhaustion of the mines—to which this town owed its origin—the inhabitants employ themselves chiefly in viz.: agriculture. CAN

CANTAL, a central department of France, formerly comprised in the prov. of Auvergne. It lies between 44° 37' and 45° 26' N lat.; and is bounded on the N by the dep. of Puy-de-Dome; on the E by that of Haute-Loire; on the SE by Lozère; on the S by Aveyron; and on the W by Lot and Corrèze. Its greatest length from NE to SW is about 60 m.; from NW to SE it is nearly of the same extent. Its superficies amounts to 571,041 hectares. This dep. is situated in the most elevated region of France, and is mountainous throughout. The greater proportion of its surface belongs to the basin of the Garonne; on the NE it enters the basin of the Loire. Nearly all its mountain-ridges are ramifications of the great central mass of the Cantal, or Plomb du Cantal, which gives name to the dep., and whose culminating point attains an elevation of 1,935 metres, or 6,348 ft. above sea-level. From this central point the greater valleys of the dep. radiate on every side into the lower country; their upper part being cut into the volcanic beds which surround the Cantal; while their lower end enters the primitive strata on which the volcanic matter has been superimposed. The principal rivers which take their rise in the Cantal are the Alagnon, a tributary of the Allier; the Trueyere and the Celle, affluents of the Lot, and the Cère, the Marone, the Auzé, the Sumène, and the Rue, tributaries of the Dordogne. The Dordogne does not enter this dep., but skirts it on the NW, separating it from Corrèze. The climate in the Mountain—as the central part of this dep. is called—is severe. Snow lies here during at least six months in the year. In the lower parts of the arrond. of Aurillac, it is at once moist and mild; in that of Mauriac, it is humid and cold; and in the remaining two dep. of Murat and Saint- Flour, it is cold and dry. -The direction of the wind is generally determined here by that of the valley through which it blows. The ecirs or snow-storms of winter are often very terrible.—The cultivation of the cerealia is not extensive in this dep. The annual production is estimated at 1,002,765 hectol. of grain; 90,480 hectol. of oats; 400,000 hectol. of potatoes; and 10,000 hectol. of wine. Not above twosevenths of the surface are under cultivation; but above one-third of the total area affords excellent pasturage, and the rearing of cattle forms an important branch of industry. The dep. is supposed to possess 16,000 horses; 175,000 horned cattle; and 372,000 sheep. A considerable number of mules and asses are also reared. The horses are small, but of a hardy active breed. Flax and hemp are extensively grown; and chestnuts are much used as an article of food. The annual territorial revenue is estimated at 10,062,000 francs; and is shared amongst 58,793 proprietaires. The imposts and public revenue in 1839 amounted to 4,662,762 francs.—The manufacturing industry of this dep. is chiefly confined to the making of leather, coarse cloth, charcoal, wooden ware, paper, and glass. In 1839, 4 coal-pits at Champacnac yielded 9,000 quintals of coal; and a lead-mine and an antimony-mine were wrought. A considerable number of the pop. annually emigrate to other dep. of France, and particularly to Paris, where they form a class somewhat similar to the Gaelic porters in the larger towns of Scotland.—There are no canals in this dep., nor navigable streams; but it is intersected by 5 government roads, of a total length of 372,803 metres; and 17 departmental roads, length 532,809 metres. The pop. in 1801 was 220,304; in 1821, 252,100; and in 1831, 258,594; in 1841, 257,323; in 1846, 260,479.—The dep. is divided into 4 arrondissements, the contract of the property of the Pop. in 1841. Pop. in 1846. Aurillac is the cap. There are only 8 towns with a pop. exceeding 3,000, viz.: Aurillac, Condat, Mauriac, Murat, Pleaux, Neuve-Eglise, Saint-Cervin, and Saint-Flour. The 4 arrond. are subdivided into 23 cant., and 265 com. The number of deputies returned under the late regime was 4; number of electors in 1846, 1,409.—The dep. forms the diocese of the bishop of Saint-Flour, suffragan of the archb. of Bourges. In 1839 it had 3 communal colleges with 342 pupils. It is under the jurisdiction of the cour royale of Riom.
CANTALAPIEDRA, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 30 m. ENE of Salamanca, on the r. bank of the Guarena, 12 m. NNE of Penaranda de Bracamonte. Pop. 1,076.
CANTALBARY, a town of Bhotan, on the l. bank of the Manshi, 90 m. SSW of Tassisudon, near the frontier of Bengal.
CANTALEJO, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 20 m. N of Segovia, and 8 m. from Sepulvedo. Pop. 1,176.

CANTALICE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo-Ultra, district and 5 m. NNW of Civita- Ducale, and 27 m. WNW of Aquila. Pop. 2,277. It is situated on a steep rock, near the base of Monte Cassuolo, and contains 4 churches, 2 convents, and a custom-house.
CANTALPINO, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 23 m. ENE of Salamanca. Pop. 1,012.

CANTALUPO, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of Sannio or Molise, district and 14 m. SE of Isernia, and 15 m. WSW of Campobasso, near the r. bank of the Biferno. Pop. 2,290. It has two annual fairs. In 1805 it was to a great extent destroyed by an earthquake. The environs afford wine in large quantities.

CANTARCA, a lake of Sicily, in a marsh of the same name, in the prov. and 30 m. S of Trapani, district and 6 m. S of Mazzara. It does not exceed 13 m. in length. A temple of Pollux formerly stood near its S bank.

CANTAVIEGA, an ancient town of Spain, in Aragon, in the prov. and 36 m. ENE of Teruel, on a hill bathed by an affluent of the Bergantes. Pop. 1,732. It possesses a parish-church, a custom-house, and a subterranean aqueduct of Roman structure.

CANTELEU, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Maromme, 4 m. W of Rouen, near the r. bank of the Seine, on the summit of a hill commanding a prospect of great extent. Pop. in 1841, 3,594. It contains a castle, and numerous villas; and has manufactories of calico, printed cottons, and a considerable commerce in timber and cyder.

CANTENAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. of Castelnau-de-Medoc, on the 1. bank of the Gironde, 12 m. N of Bordeaux. Pop. 853. It is celebrated for its wines, especially for the species known as the Gorse. It possesses a small port, the trade of which consists chiefly in the exportation of wine and timber.

CANTERBURY, the metropolitan see of all England, and a city and county in itself, locally situated in the hund. of Bridge and Petham, eastern division of the co. of Kent, in N lat. 51° 17', E long. 1° 15', 55 m. ESE of London, on the river Stour, and 6 m. distant from the port of Whitstable, with which it is connected by a railway. Pop. of the city and pre- CAN 14,463; in 1841, 15,435. Area 3,204 acres. C. is pleasantly situated in a valley surrounded by hills. the ruins of St. Augustine's monastery,—the lesser spires of the churches, -and the W gate of the city, -to the verdant hills surrounding the whole. It is of an oval shape; and extends, within the walls, about half-a-mile from E to W, and somewhat more from N to S. The wall extends in circumf. about 13 m. Great improvements have been made in the general appearance of C. within the last few years; it is now well-paved, lighted with gas, and furnished with a good supply of water from the river Stour; and an efficient police has been established. The city stands on nearly the same spot on which it was originally built, as is evident from the remains of antiquity which have been at various periods discovered here. Roman bricks, tesselated pavements, vases, lachrymatories, and personal ornaments, have frequently been found here, at about 6 to 9 ft. beneath the surface of the ground. At what period C. was first walled is not certain; but that it was enclosed in the time of the Anglo-Saxons is evident, from mention being made of the wall prior to the Norman conquest. The ditch that surrounded the walls was originally 150 ft. wide; the greater part of it, at the present time, is built upon, or converted into gardens. The arches which carried the wall over the river have been taken down; and of the six gates that formed the principal entrances, only the W gate is standing. It consists of a centre flanked by two round towers. The streets, the principal of which are four in number, intersect each other at right angles. In some parts of the city, the houses have still the projecting upper stories, and the narrow streets of the 14th cent.; but these features of the olden time are rapidly disappearing before the progress of modern improvement. The environs are pleasant, and the surrounding scenery is agreeably diversified. On the road to the Isle of Thanet, barracks for cavalry, infantry, and artillery have been erected on an extensive scale.—The ruins of St. Augustine's abbey were situated at Longport, in the E suburb of the city. A missionary college is now erected on the spot, in connexion with the established church. The buildings form a quadrangle. The E side of the quadrangle is occupied by the library and museum, the latter forming the ground story, the roof being groined and supported by circular shafts of stone. The library is a fine room, with an oak roof. The buildings on the left side comprise the chapel, dining-hall, and warden's house. The west end of the chapel is a restoration of the original structure. The windows are filled with stained glass, and the floor paved with coloured tiles. The library and museum are of undressed Kentish rag, in irregular courses, with Caen stone dressings. The buttresses of the chapel are of square flint, and the whole of the other buildings of flint and Caen stone. - Previously to the dissolution of the religious houses, C. had no trade nor any manufacture except that of relics, but depended entirely upon the pilgrims who came to worship at the shrine of its patron saint, Thomas à Becket. The depopulation that ensued at the Reformation prepared the city for the reception of the persecuted Walloons and French protestants, who found here a ready reception and a grateful asylum, bringing along with them a useful art, that of silk-weaving. The ingenuity and diligence of these industrious refugees restored in some degree the prosperity of the city, which now became the principal seat of the manufacture of silk in Engcincts in 1801, 9,000; in 1821, 12,745; in 1831, land. The silk-manufacture gave place in its turn to that of cotton and silk; but this branch of trade has ceased. Some trade, however, is still carried on in wool. There are numerous mills on the river. The markets for all kinds of agricultural produce are well kept up; and great quantities of hops, wool, and flour are sent to London. The neighbourhood of C. is chiefly planted with hops.—Frequent attempts have been made to improve the navigation of the Stour. The formation of a railway to Whitstable, whence there is water-conveyance to London, led to the abandonment of the attempts to improve the river. The railway runs nearly in a straight line from North Lane to Whitstable, where it terminates at the harbour. It is held in 1,172 shares of £50 each, and has been converted into a locomotive line in connexion with the C. branch of the South-eastern railway, which was opened on 6th February, 1846. This branch line leaves the main trunk at Ashford station, 663 m. from London, and passes through a richly-wooded and picturesque country. Its length is 14 m.; and an extension of it to Ramsgate and Margate is in progress.—By the new municipal act the city has been divided into three wards, to each of which 6 councillors have been assigned, and incorporated under the title of 'The mayor and commonalty of the city of Canterbury.' The revenue of the borough, in 1837, was £7,746. The expenditure was £7,140.—The county gaol and house-of-correction, erected in 1808, on the radiating plan, with the keeper's house and chapel in the centre, adjoins the court-house, in which are held the quarter-sessions for East Kent.—Since the 23d of Edward I., C. has returned two members to parliament. The 40s. freeholders in this city now vote for the county of Kent. The number of electors registered for 1836-7 was 1,835, of whom 1,152 were freemen, and 633 householders; in 1847, the number was 1,625. Archbishopric.] During the time of its occupation by the Romans. C. was visited by the heralds of the cross, and so early as the 2d. cent. this city could boast of two Christian churches. Becoming afterwards the metropolis of the Saxon kingdom of Kent, one of these churches was appropriated for the use of Bertha, the Christian queen of Ethelbert. Pope Gregory, about this time, sent the missionary Augustine to England, who took up his station with Queen Bertha, at C., and made a convert of Ethelbert. Gregory rewarded Augustine's services by investing him with the dignity of archbishop, constituting C. the seat of his see; and from that day to this, this city has claimed and enjoyed metropolitan dignity, under a succession of 92 archbishops. As metropolitan of all England, the archb. of C. ranks as first peer of the realm, taking precedence, with the exception of the royal family, of all the nobility and chief officers-of-state. At coronations, he places the crown upon the sovereign's head; and, wherever the court may be, the king and queen are deemed his parishioners. The b. of London is his provincial dean; the b. of Winchester his sub-dean; the b. of Lincoln his chancellor; and the b. of Rochester his chaplain. In right of his primacy, he is a privy councillor; and he has the power of conferring degrees in the several faculties of divinity, law, and physic, except within the immediate jurisdiction of the universities of Oxford and Cambridge. His province comprehends the sees of 22 suffraganbishops; his diocese comprises 258 parishes in the county of Kent, and nearly 100 parishes in that and other counties are in his peculiar jurisdiction. The deaneries under the see are 10, namely, C., Bridge, Charing, Dover, Elham, Lympne, Ospringe, Sandwich, Sittingbourne, Sutton, and Westbere. In 1834, the gross revenue of the archbishop was £22,216; that of the dean and chapter, £21,551. The cathedral of C. exhibits specimens in every style of architecture, from the earliest Norman down to the latest English. It is situated in the N part of the city, and covers a great extent of ground. Its form is that of a double cross, with a tower 235 ft, in height, in the later style of English architecture, with a pierced parapet and pinnacles, and having octagonal turrets at the angles, terminating in minarets rising from the intersection of the nave and the western transepts. At the west end are two massive towers, both in the Norman style. The whole length of the cathedral from E to W is 514 ft. within the walls. The length of the choir, by far the most spacious in the kingdom, is 180 ft. The length of the eastern transept is 154, and the length of the western 124 ft. The breadth, including the aisles, is 71, and the height of the vaulted roof 80 ft.

CANTERBURY, a settlement on the E coast of the Middle island of New Zealand, in the Port Cooper district, now in course of organization under the aus- CAN pices of an influential committee of Episcopalians. It is a level district, watered by numerous rivulets and streams, extending coastwise to the N and SW, and bounded inland by a range of mountains whose distance from the coast varies from 40 to 50 m. The estimated extent of plain land is 1,765,000 acres; of woodland, 237,100 acres; of mountain and hill land, 397,900 acres. Total, 2,400,000 acres. The soil is a light loam, resting on gravel and blue clay, affording excellent natural pasturage, and capable of yielding good crops of all kinds of European grain, potatoes, fruits, and vegetables. The surface rises gradually from the coast to the foot of the mountains, where it attains an alt. of about 500 ft. above sea-level. The mountains themselves are generally capped with snow, and probably attain an alt. of from 7,000 to 8,000 ft. The rivers flow to the S of E.; and in succession from N to S have been named Farquhar, Ashley, Eyre, Courtenay, Hawkins, Selwyn, Cholmondeley, Wynne, and Ashburton. On the coast, immediately to the W of Banks's peninsula, is a large lake or lagune, communicating with the sea, called Waihora by the natives, but which has received the name of Lake Ellesmere. The fine harbour of Port Cooper in the NW angle of Banks's peninsula will be the market and harbour of this promising colony. It is understood that the block of land above described was purchased of the natives for £2,000; and that a charter has been granted by the Crown; that the first body of colonists will be composed of such persons as may become purchasers of land on or before the 30th of April, 1850; that no block of land can be purchased of a less size than 50 acres; and that the sum to be paid for rural land is £3 per acre, for a half-acre allotment in the intended capital, Christchurch, £24, and for a quarteracre allotment in other towns, £12. It is calculated that when the whole territory shall be disposed of, the money received for the purchase will form a fund of £1,000,000 for religious and educational purposes; £1,000,000 for emigration; and £500,000 for miscellaneous purposes.

CANTERBURY, a township of Merrimac co., in the state of New Hampshire, U. S., 8 m. N of Concord, bounded on the W by the Merrimac, which is here crossed by two bridges. The surface is hilly, but the soil generally good. Pop. in 1840, 1,643.— Also a township of Windham co., in the state of Connecticut, 12 m. N of Norwich. On the E it is bounded by the Quinebang, which abounds with shad. It possesses an undulating surface, and a fertile soil. Pop. 1,791.—Also a village of Cornwall township, Orange co., in the state of New York, on Hudson river, 1 m. W of Cornwall Landing, and 89 m. S of Albany. Pop. 500.

CANTIANO, a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the delegation of Urbino and Pesaro, 20 m. S of Urbino, on the r. bank of a river—an affluent of the Metaura—of the same name. Pop. 2,500. It occupies the site of the ancient Luccola.
CANTILLANA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 17 m. NE of Seville, on the Guadalquiver, 15 m. SE of Lora-del-Rio. Pop. 4,121. It contains a parish church and a custom-house.
CANTIMPELOS, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 9 m. N of Segovia.
CANTIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Arleux, 4 m. SE of Douai. Pop. 806. It possesses several chemical-works.
CANTIN (CAPE), or RAS-AL-HOUDIK, a headland of Marocco, on the W coast, in N lat. 32° 35', W long. 9° 15'. A little to the SE is a village of the same name.
CANTIVEROS, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 27 m. NNW of Avila, at some distance from the Zapardiel.

CANTLEY, a parish of Norfolk, 4½ m. SSW of i Acle, on the Yare. Area 2,970 acres. Pop. in 1841, 210.—Also a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. SE of Doncaster, near the Idle, on the Great North road. Area 5,160 acres. Pop. in 1841, 651.
CANTO, a town of Cochin China, in the prov. of Cambodia, on the l. bank of the Japanese river, 50 m. NW of Miltho, and 45 m. W of Saigun.
CANTOBRE, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. and com. of Nant, 14 m. SE of Millau. Pop. 180. Coal is wrought in the environs.

CANTOIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Sainte-Genevieve. Pop. 1,624. CANTON, KWANG-CHOO-FOO, or SING-CHING, a large and maritime city of China, the capital of the prov. of Kwang-tung, on the N bank of the river Choo- Keang, or Tigris, in N lat. 23° 7′ 10", and E long. 113° 14' 30", about 40 m. above the Bocca Tigris, 80 m. from Macao, and in 3° 31' W of Pekin. It is fortified by a strong rampart between 6 and 7 m. in circumf.; and consists of three divisions separated from each other by thick walls running E and W, which, together with the rampart, are built of brick and stone, are from 25 ft. to 40 ft. in height, and 25 ft. in thickness, and covered with trees and plants. The N, or larger division, is called the old city; and is occupied by the Tartar pop., and the Mantchou garrison. The S division is the new city; but the suburbs are more extensive than either of the walled divisions. C. is defended toward the water by two high walls, and two strong castles built in the middle of the river, which is wider than the Thames at London; on the land-side, by a strong wall and forts. On the N side, this wall rests directly on the brow of the hills, and its base is between 200 and 300 ft. above the river. The city is entered by 12 outer iron gates, in each of which there is a guard-house. Within these gates no European is admitted, and no one is allowed to pass in or out during the night. Not above one-third of the ground within the walls, however, is filled with building; the rest being chiefly appropriated to pleasure-grounds and fish-ponds. The back or northern part of the city is almost entirely occupied by two hills of considerable height, which are beautifully wooded, and are cultivated with great care. As Europeans have not yet been freely allowed to enter within the gates, our accounts of C. are confined principally to its suburbs. The streets in these quarters - which differ little if at all from those within the walls-are in general long, but very narrow and irregular, paved with small round stones, and flagged with large slabs chiefly of granite close to the houses. They are ornamented at intervals with arches; and are kept exceedingly clean; some of them have a kind of awning extended from house to house, which prevents the sun's rays from incommoding the inhabitants. Opulent individuals live in large buildings not unlike Chinese palaces; but the houses are in general low; or, if belonging to rich individuals, are surrounded by a wall. So strict is the regard had to privacy among the Chinese, that their houses-excepting only shops-have no windows to the front in the street; and none of them are overlooked in other parts of the premises; even their entrances have screens to prevent the interior court being seen when the gates are opened. They have no chimneys, but the rooms are warmed with iron pots of ignited charcoal. The shops sometimes consist of several apartments, of which the one next the street is generally quite open in front, and filled with coarse porcelain and toys; in the one behind, a finer sort of porcelain is sold; the third contains silks, velvets, and other kinds of cloth; and some have a fourth, where tea or other commodities of that description are kept. On great festivals, these long narrow vaults are thrown open, when they are illuminated with lamps, and ornamented with flowers and trees. Most of the merchants, however, are content with two apartments; the outer one for exhibiting their porcelain wares, and the other for articles of every description. Some of the streets in the suburbs are appropriated entirely to particular trades, especially cabinet-makers and painters; but in others, apothecaries, shoemakers, goldsmiths, tailors, bookbinders, &c., are all mingled together. In one very long street, nothing but eggs are sold; and the accumulation of millions of eggs in every shop presents a very singular appearance. Several canals run up from the river to the interior of the city, on which boats with merchandise are continually passing and repassing. When any of these cross a street, it is covered in by an arch which is ascended on each side by a flight of 12 or 14 steps. A large part of the city and suburbs is built on low ground or flats: special care, therefore, is requisite in order to secure for houses and temples a solid basis. Near the river, and in all the most loose or muddy situations, houses are raised on wooden piles, which make the foundation as secure as brick or stone, and perhaps more so. In some cases, the piles rise above the surface of the ground, and then the buildings, constructed of wood, rest directly on them; but in other instances, the piles reach only within a few feet of the surface, and the remaining part of the foundation is made of mud, brick, or stone. When this is done, the walls of the houses are usually carried up and completed with the same material. Not a few of the houses are entirely baseless, or have only a slender foundation of mud, of which also their walls are composed; and hence, in severe rain-storms and overflowings of the river, many of the houses are prostrated. Bricks are in most general use for the walls of houses; perhaps three-fifths of the whole city are built of this material; of the remaining part, a very large portion is constructed of mud: most of the Tartars in the old city inhabit houses of this description. Stone and wood are not very extensively used for the walls of houses; the first is frequently employed about gateways and for door-posts; and the second for columns, beams, and rafters. Many of the floors of the houses and temples are formed of indurated mud; marble flags are sometimes used for the same purpose, and often tiles. The latter, when made very thin, are used for roofs; they are laid on the rafters in rows alternately concave and convex, forming ridges and furrows, and luted by a cement of clay. Windows are small, and rarely supplied with glass; paper, mica, or shell, or some other similar translucent substance, taking its place. Very little iron is employed in building houses. Such is the general style, and the usual material of the buildings of C. In passing through the streets of the city, the spectator is struck with the difference he finds in its various buildings: though this diversity does by no means fully exhibit the relative condition and circumstances of the people. A few only are rich; and the external appearance of their houses does not at all exceed in elegance those of the middling class. Many are very poor; and the aspect of their habitations exhibits abundant evidence of their abject state. The poorest people are to be found in the extreme parts of the suburbs, along the banks of the canals, and in the N part of the old city. Their houses are mere mud hovelslow, narrow, dark, uncleanly, and without any division of apartments. A whole family of six, eight, ten, and sometimes twice that number of individuals, is crowded into one of these dreary abodes. In habitations a little more spacious and cleanly than these, perhaps one-third of the population of C. have their dwelling. These stand close on the streets; and have usually but a single entrance, which is closed by a bamboo screen suspended from the top of the door. Within these houses there are no superfluous apartments; a single room, allotted to each branch of the family, serves for a dormitory; while a third, which completes the number into which the whole enclosure is divided, is used by all the household as a common eating room. Chinese houses usually open towards the south; but in these, as also in the poorer kind, this favourite position is disregarded. Houses of this description are rented at four or five dollars a month. [Chinese Repository.] All burdens are carried by porters, who in general go barefoot and bareheaded; a horse is rarely seen in this city, and the only vehicles for both sexes are palanquins, which are chairs similar to our sedanchairs, but more elegantly decorated, and carried by poles supported on men's shoulders. The streets are daily crowded with an immense concourse of people, which renders it difficult for a stranger to make his way through them, and his ears are continually stunned with the cries of the porters, who, passing along at a round pace, keep bawling out Li! li! that is, 'Clear the way!' yet you seldom see a woman in them, unless by chance, when one may be coming out of her chair. When a mandarin passes through the streets it is under the imposing form of a procession, before which all people give way, tumbling over one another, to escape the chains which are otherwise thrown over their necks, and by which they are dragged to the punishment of the bamboo and bastinado; his worship, the mandarin, directing the punishment, and ordering the number of blows. It is a rare thing indeed for a house to be broken open by thieves, or for a murder to be committed in C.; this is the result of a degree of exactness and order in all ranks, which is miraculous to a European. There are corps de garde in every street, night and day, -some armed with swords, others with whips, ready to visit all disturbers of the peace with summary punishment; the power to take in charge is used only in graver offences. No persons are suffered to go about at night, except on express and well explained occasions. Yet, as we shall have occasion to notice in the course of this article, the C. mob is often peculiarly turbulent. "Fires are frequent in C., and are usually caused by incendiaries, who rob and plunder, during the attendant confusion with impunity. In 1842, three of the factories were burnt, and 95,000 Chinese buildings. From the nature of the building materials used, fire spreads with fearful rapidity; and as there is a paucity of water, the destruction of life and property, when a fire occurs, is most terrific. In 1844, a theatre fire took place, and thirty-three adjacent buildings were consumed; and it is stated by Chinese authorities that 2,300 human beings fell a sacrifice to the devouring element. The punishment inflicted upon the viceroy by the laws of China, when a fire occurs within his province, is curious. If more than 10 houses are consumed, he forfeits as many months' salary; if 31 houses are destroyed, then he loses one year's pay; but if 301 houses are burnt, then he loses caste, being degraded to the rank below the one he occupies. But all these penalties are only enforced if the fire happens in the city, as conflagrations in the suburbs entail no punishment on the viceroy." [Sirr.] The principal buildings in C. are joss houses, or temples, which are, to the number of 124, scattered over all the city and suburbs. These contain the images of Chinese worship, before which are placed, at particular seasons, a great variety of sweetmeats, dressed food, and incense, which is kept burning. The Buddhist temples are the most numerous. The number of priests is said to amount to 3,000; that of Chinese force brought to the factories, attempting to 11.

CANTON. nuns to 1,000; and the mendicant friars are numerous. There is also a Mahommedan mosque in the city; and in one of the factories is a British chapel. The palace of the Tsong-too, or viceroy, is rather a handsome building, but quite in the Chinese taste, and disfigured with the images of monstrous animals. In the environs there is a regular theatre, in a large square or market-place. The most beautiful part of C., however, is the quay which contains the European factories, which "are perhaps the best buildings in the whole empire." [Gutzlaff.] These extend a considerable way along the banks of the river in the NW suburbs, and, previous to their destruction during the late war with the Chinese, consisted of a range of elegant buildings, with yards and warehouses, each having the flag of the nation to which it belongs hoisted before the gate from sunrise to sunset. These buildings are occupied by the supercargoes of their respective companies, who transact all the business with the Chinese merchants, disposing of the cargoes brought to market, and supplying the ships with others for Europe in return. The treaty of Nankin has, it would appear, failed to improve the social position of foreigners in the C. factories. The right of entry to C., and of proceeding to a reasonable distance into the country beyond it, for exercise and relaxation, had been guaranteed by the treaty, but was subsequently surrendered by her Majesty's plenipotentiary, in deference to the alleged inability of the Chinese government to control the people. The C. residents blame the British authorities very much for this. "The policy pursued by her Majesty's plenipotentiary and the subordinate consular officers appears," they state, in a petition to parliament, "to be characterised on all occasions by subserviency to the Chinese authorities, by a ready submission to injuries inflicted by them on British subjects, and by an over eagerness to listen to complaints and to punish with severity when British subjects are supposed to have been the offenders." The annoyances of which the C. residents complain, are attributed by the witnesses before a committee of the house of commons, to the peculiarly hostile and turbulent disposition of the people, to their great dislike to foreigners, and to the feebleness and weakness of the local authorities. Captain Balfour, a government employé in China, says, "the C. people have always been noted in China as a turbulent race; they resisted the Tartar dynasty more than any other people in China." Mr. Gibb, who has been settled in China some fourteen years, also attributes all the grievances to the people, and to the weakness of the authorities in controlling them. "I have seen gentlemen," he says, "who were walking quietly round the city, stoned by the inhabitants. Mr. M. was robbed and stoned in going round C. last year with the vice-consul." This gentleman declares, that to the best of his knowledge the misunderstandings have always arisen on the side of the Chinese. It seems, indeed, from the evidence, that the populace object to the foreigners going beyond the limits of their factories at all. "At C.," says one witness, "we dare not go 100 yards from the factory. Immediately you get within 10 or 20 yards of the gates of the city, you have a mob round you." It was usual for the Europeans to visit a great temple across the water, in what are called the Fantee gardens, but it seems that it is not now safe to go across the river, in consequence of the mob being so excited. The river at C. is described to be about the breadth of the Thames at Westminster; but, in consequence of the number of boats lying at the banks, the navigable space is not greater than that of the same river at Putney. Another witness says, "I have seen the control the mob, and the mob has driven away the Chinese authorities, even in the presence of the mandarins. The mob of C. was, in fact, stronger than the government force." In another instance the mob were permitted to attack the factories for an hour and a half before there was any interference by the authorities; and on another occasion the house of the governor of the city was attacked and burnt by the mob, despite all the efforts of the mandarins. Mr. Rawson, a merchant of C., complains of the very limited space that British subjects are permitted to occupy. "Our community since the war in 1838 has," he says, "increased I should think tenfold; but we are even more limited in space in C. for residences than we were in former years. We have not above 400 yards square. Since the war there is only one part of the original factories left; the British, Dutch, Spanish, and two or three other hongs have been destroyed by fire. The ground is held by lease, which the British government take from the Chinese authorities; but the space is so confined, and the applications for land so numerous, that there is not the slightest piece of ground available even for landing goods. The consequence is that the merchants are obliged to use the stores of the Chinese, the insecurity of which has been shown by two or three recent failures of native merchants, who were proved to have been pawning the goods thus consigned to their custody." This neglect to provide sufficient space appears to be Nankin, by which the Chinese are bound to supply, <math>| \text{ in } n \text{ n.} </math> H About a league above C. is 'the Boat-town,' which consists of about 40,000 barks of various sizes, arranged in straight rows, and forming a floating city, with regular streets. Each bark is covered, and lodges a whole family. The number of persons who are expressly prohibited by the law from settling on shore, and are thus obliged to live upon the water, may amount to nearly 300,000, including the women of the town, who are estimated at 40,000. They are chiefly composed of the poorer sort of Chinese, or rather the descendants of the Tartars. Every person's boat is his home, containing his children, poultry, and domestic animals. The boats are built with at reasonable rates, any ground that may be required for residences or commercial purposes, or even for building a church. "So far from this being done," says Mr. Rawson, "at this moment the rent is nearly 1,000 dollars for houses which let at 100 dollars before the war; and with regard to a church, a private subscription has been raised in C. to provide a salary for a clergyman, but no space can be found whereon to build a church in which he can officiate." The most immediate measure of protection to the residents at C. is suggested by different witnesses to be the stationing of a British vessel of war opposite the factories. C. itself lies too far back from the river to be under the command of any guns from a ship of war; but the hongs are on the river's bank, and the guns of the steamer would command such parts of the river as are available for recreation and for the use of the residents." "Throughout the whole of this large floating city, the greatest regularity prevails; the large boats are arranged in rows, forming streets, through which the small craft pass and repass, like coaches and other vehicles in a large town. The families who live in this manner seem to have a great partiality for flowers, which they keep in pots, either upon the high stern of their boats, or in their little parlours. The Chinese Arbor vitæ, Gardenias, cycas, revoluta, cockscombs, and oranges seem to be the greatest favourites with them. A joss-house—small, indeed, in many cases, but yet arched covers, high enough to walk under; and are, an altar—is indispensable to all these floating-houses. Here the joss-stick and oil are daily burned, and form the incense which these poor people offer to their imaginary deity." [Athenœum.] The beds are fixed below the decks, which constitute the housefloors, as it were, and are removable. In the hinder part of the vessel is the store, which is used for all cooking purposes; with one considerable apartment for live animals only, especially ducks and waterfowl, which go in and out of their own floating homes with an instinct more surprising than their docility on land. Mandarins in their barges go up and down the river, as they do in processions in the city, looking after, controlling, and regulating the community of the waters as their especial jurisdiction. "Whole streets of wooden houses are also built upon the sides of the river and the numerous canals in the suburbs of C., upon stakes which are driven firmly into the mud. These dwellings resemble the travelling-shows which one often sees in the market-towns of England; suppose that instead of the wheels which these vehicles generally have, they were supported upon posts over water, and crowded together in hundreds and thousands, forming crooked and irregular streets, then you have a good idea of the dwelling-houses of the Chinese on the C. river. Thousands of the inhabitants live and enjoy health and happiness in such places, which, according to our ideas, would soon be graves for Europeans-such is the difference of constitution." [Athenœum.] As C. is still the chief emporium of European commerce in the empire, a full account of its trade will be given under the article China. Its principal article of exportation is tea. In 1795, while the English private trade in this commodity amounted to 23,733,810 lbs., the other nations of Europe and America received only 5,577,200 lbs., of which 4,096,800 lbs. were exported in Dutch vessels. All European countries (with the exception of Russia) and America have hitherto drawn their supplies of tea from the Eastern tea districts through the port of C. It appears that the quantities of tea retained for home consumption in the United Kingdom amounted, in 1740, to 1,493,625 lbs., and in 1844 (a century afterwards) to no less than 41,363,770 lbs. In the period between 1740 and 1766, the consumption varied from 473,868 lbs. to 5,307,292 lbs. annually; between 1767 and 1783, from 3,776,229 lbs. to 7,328,098 lbs. annually; between 1784 and 1794, from 10,159,701 lbs. to 18,665,365 lbs. annually; between 1795 and 1805, from 18,858,427 lbs. to 25,400,294 lbs. annually; between 1806 and 1818, from 21,065,843 lbs. to 26,527,531 lbs. annually; between 1819 and 1833, from 25,241,693 lbs. to 31,829,620 lbs. annually; between 1834 and 1837, it averaged 37,827,774 lbs. annually. In 1840, it amounted to 22,576,405 lbs.; in 1842, to 37,409,544 lbs.; and in 1844, to 52,179,533 lbs. The country around C. is pleasant and healthy; and the approach to the city by water, presents the most beautiful and picturesque scenery that can be imagined. In the background, on the N and NE sides, high and fantastically shaped mountains raise their summits among the clouds, while all around, with little exception, to the foot of these mountains—which form the key to the occupation of the city—the ground seems a level verdant plain intersected with innumerable branches of the river, and artificial canals, which serve both for irrigation and for communication with the interior. It is this last circumstance that renders the scenery of the Tigris or C. river so truly picturesque. "The sea, near its mouth, is studded all over with numerous islands, of which a good view is obtained in going over from Hong-Kong to Macao; and in sailing from either of these places to C. we pass a succession of them, most of which are mountainous, having huge masses of rock and yellow gravelly clay protruding here and there all over the surface, and but thinly covered with vegetation of any kind. Sometimes, however, in our progress we have views of beautiful bays with a few acres of level land near the shore, in the midst of which there are some pretty houses or huts surrounded with a few trees and shrubs. These quiet villages abound with pirates, who frequently commit acts of the most cold-blooded cruelty, and render the passages between Hong-Kong, C., and Macao, unpleasant and dangerous. Lorchas having a valuable cargo on board, are frequently attacked, and the crew and passengers murdered for the sake of the contents of the vessel, which is generally ransacked, and afterwards burned or destroyed. Large quantities of rice are grown, both on the islands formed by the river, and on the flats on the mainland; the tide is kept out by embankments, where of course the ground can be overflowed at will. These embankments are not allowed to be idle, but are made to produce crops of plantains, as well as to preserve the ground from the inundations of the tide. When the land is too high to be overflowed by the tide, the water-wheel is brought into play. A great number of the common fruit-trees of the country are also grown all over the plains and near the side of the river. Large quantities of the water lily, or lotus, are grown, both below and above the city, near the sides of the river, and the plots containing them are embanked in the same manner as the rice-fields." The garrison of C. consists of about 20,000 Tarries, 7,000. About 17,000 individuals, women and sizes belonging to the district, and trading along the river between C. and Whampoa. In the city and suburbs of C. there are 1,600 houses of ill fame, which pay each a monthly license to the police of 200 Spanish dollars. It is known by the issue of permits, that there are daily slaughtered in the city of C. 24,000 pigs. The five principal districts contain each about 1,000 unpaid police; the middle sized ditto, 300 to 400; and the smallest, 100 to 200; all these gain a livelihood by preventing, conniving at, or encouraging crime. There are never less than from 8,000 to 10,000 trading-vessels lying before the city; independent of the smaller fry of the boat-craft, trafficking in provisions. The mandarin pleasureboats are the stars of the river; which is thronged with vessels of every grade and quality, from C. down to Whampoa, where the foreign vessels lie. Among the trading classes there are numbers who can talk broken English and Portuguese, but no other European language, so that the French, Dutch, and Danes, are obliged to carry on their intercourse in one or the other of these tongues. According to Raper, the average temperature of this city in 1774 was 75° 4'; the greatest heat in January being 66° 5', and the least 51°; the greatest heat in August 89°, and the least 80°. In the winter season there is sometimes a difference in the temp. of 20° or 25° between morning and noon. Though C. is but 24° from the equator, and is scorchingly hot in summer, in December and January it is subject to high winds, and heavy rains: the Chinese then take to their winter-dresses, lined with furs or quilted cotton. Fans are equally used by men, women, and children; and it is not unusual for persons in cold weather to carry live quails in their hands for warmth. During the heavy rains, the streets of C. are almost impassable; and the inhabitants, in many quarters, scull small boats for the purposes of intercourse. History.] The province of Kwang-tung was known under various names at an early period of Chinese history, as a barbarous and uncivilized country; but it was not till about 220 B. C., that the Chinese monarchs began to turn their attention towards it. Tsin-che-kwang, the founder of the Tsin dynasty, and the first universal monarch of China, pushed his conquests to the S of the Meiling chain, which bounds this prov. on the N; and rendered tributary to the empire the whole country of Yuc and Min, as Canton, Foh-kien, Cochin-china, and the surrounding kingdoms were then called. After his death Chaowto, an assistant of the aged viceroy of C.—which then included under its government the provinces of the Kwangse and Yunan, and the kingdoms of Cochin-china and Tonquin-assumed the regal title, but soon after retired into Cochin-china. During his continuance at C. he is said to have built, as a palace or pleasure house, a remarkable edifice called the seven-stoned pagoda, which is still to be seen on a hill to the N of the city. When the founder of the Han dynasty succeeded in overthrowing the usurper of the imperial throne, and in his turn took the title of emperor, Chou-to was compelled to relinquish the authority which he had assumed in Canton and Kwangse, and in his capa city of king of Cochin-china and Tonquin, acknowledge himself a tributary of the Chinese emperors. C. now became the place of banishment for disgraced statesmen. Even at this early period, the city began to show symptoms of the luxury and extravagance which have since characterised it; for its inhabitants received a severe reprimand from the emperor for having sent him a present of fine cloth, and a mandate was issued forbidding them to manufacture any more such cloth in future. The Chinese who first entered this and the neighbouring provinces to the W and NW, drove the natives, who were probably aborigines, into fastnesses in the mountains, where they have ever since remained under the independent government of their own chieftains, and whence they have often issued to attack their lowland neighbours. These mountaineers are known under the general denomination of Meaoutse, but they are likewise distinguished, in various places, by different epithets, all of a contemptuous nature. The Chinese have frequently carried on destructive wars against them, but have never been able entirely to subdue them. It was about the 7th cent. that, as Chinese annals relate, vessels first brought rare commodities to C. from foreign countries: soon after, at the commencement of the 8th cent., the famous pass through the Meiling mountains was cut, in order to facilitate intercourse between C. and the more northern provinces of China. It was probably at this period that the Arabians and Moors first began to trade with China, as we find that during this and the following centuries there were, according to the accounts of two Arabian travellers, a very large number of Mohammedan traders, from Shiraz, Bussorah, &c., resident at Canfu or Kwan-chow-foo. The descendants of these Mohammedans are still numerous in C.; but of the Christians and Jews also mentioned by travellers, no traces have ever, with any degree of certainty, been found. Whilst the Sung dynasty occupied the throne of China, from the 10th to the 12th cent., C. enjoyed great prosperity. In the 13th cent., when the Mongul dynasty had overpowered the Sung dynasty, the travels of Marco Polo, in the N of China, first informed Europeans of the existence of that country; but the trade with the East being then in the hands of the Mohammedans, it was seldom visited by any of the nations of Christendom, until the passage of the Cape of Good Hope by the Portuguese, upwards of 200 years afterwards, had opened a road for ships from Europe to China. The first Europeans who visited China by sea were the Portuguese. In 1516 a vessel which had been despatched from this country by Alfonso Albuquerque, captain - general of Malacca, reached the islands in front of the Canton river; and, her voyage being altogether a successful one, she was followed next year by a fleet of 8 vessels. But as the trade increased, the good opinion which, during the first year or two, the Chinese had been led to form of their new acquaintances, was lessened, and the jealousy of the Chinese was also excited by hearing of their assumption of territory in India and the Malayan archipelago. Hence, in 1520, we find a Portuguese ambassador to the court of Pekin sent back, without having gained any advantage, and eventually imprisoned and slain. After the Portuguese, the Spanish, Dutch, English, and other nations, followed successively and by their mutual strifes and jealousies, only increased the contempt in which the Chinese had been already taught to hold all foreigners. Yet, notwithstanding their repeated declarations of indifference to foreign commerce, it appears that the Chinese were, even at that time, by no means ignorant of the advantages to be derived from it. At different times the ports of C., Macao, and Teenpih were severally opened to Europeans in this prov.; while Ningpo and Chusan, in Chekiang, and Amoy, in Fohkien, also became large marts for their commerce; but soon after the conquest of China by the present Mantchou dynasty, "the nations of the western ocean" were required to confine their trade to the ports of C. and Macao alone. The English first turned their attention to China in 1596, but the expedition then equipped proved unfortunate, the vessels being lost on their passage out. The next attempt to establish a trade here was in 1637, about 20 years later than our first intercourse with India; but the opposition of the Portuguese for many years prevented the success of this and several other expeditions. In 1676, the East India company succeeded in establishing a factory at Amoy, from which they were driven away in 1680, but in 1784, they were permitted to return to their factory; where they remained until foreign commerce became confined to C. and Macao. In 1785 the first vessel fitted out from the United States arrived at C. The principal event in the history of C., since the commencement of this century, was its capture by the British force in May 1840, when the whole of the river-defences, and the heights above the city, were taken possession of, and held until the payment of 6,000,000 dollars.- See article China.
